title: High-throughput study of electrical conductivity in ordered metals
authors: Thalis H. B. da Silva, Hai-Chen Wang, Tiago F. T. Cerqueira, Simone Di Cataldo, Silvana Botti
abstract: We present a computational framework that integrates machine learning with high-throughput \textit{ab initio} calculations to screen over 2.8 million compounds for metallic transport. We identify several intermetallic candidates with predicted high conductivities comparable to that of aluminum ($36.59 \times 10^6$~S/m). We perform full electron--phonon coupling calculations for the top-performing materials, yielding results in excellent agreement with available experimental data. Our analysis reveals that while the noble metals (Ag, Au, Cu) define the practical ceiling for conductivity due to their unique electronic structure and low scattering, compounds like $\text{LiBePt}_2$ can achieve comparable performance by utilizing valence electrons from light elements to shift high-scattering $d$-states beneath the Fermi level. This study not only identifies novel high-performance conductors but also demonstrates the predictive power of combining statistical learning with detailed ab initio calculations.
summary: Researchers developed a high-throughput framework combining machine learning with first-principles calculations to screen 2.8 million metallic compounds for high electrical conductivity. They identified new intermetallic candidates that could rival aluminum's conductivity by using light elements to reduce electron-phonon scattering. The study demonstrates that ML can effectively bridge the gap between massive chemical searches and expensive, high-accuracy quantum mechanical simulations.
main_problem: The challenge of efficiently predicting and optimizing electrical conductivity in metals across a massive chemical space of millions of compounds.
main_result: The study identifies several intermetallic candidates, such as LiBePt2, with predicted conductivities comparable to aluminum, and demonstrates that machine learning can accurately screen 2.8 million compounds.
method: A multi-stage high-throughput workflow integrating machine learning surrogate models (ALIGNN) with ab initio calculations (DFT, BoltzTraP2, and EPW) to predict conductivity and electron-phonon coupling.
model_or_system: The study screens approximately 2.8 million ordered inorganic metallic compounds from the Alexandria database, focusing on the interplay between electronic structure and electron-phonon scattering.
key_observables: Electrical conductivity (sigma), electron-phonon coupling constant (lambda), Fermi velocity (vF), and carrier density.
important_parameters: Distance to the convex hull (E_hull) <= 25 meV/atom for stability; conductivity threshold > 15 x 10^6 S/m.
main_assumptions: The initial large-scale screening uses the constant relaxation-time approximation, which neglects material-specific scattering variations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the computational workflow; Figure 2 illustrates the distribution of predicted conductivity vs. electron-phonon coupling; Figure 3 validates ML predictions against DFT; Figure 4 shows the fit of a derived empirical formula to experimental data.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the computational challenge, describes the multi-stage ML and ab initio workflow, presents the screening results and physical insights into high-conductivity mechanisms, and validates the models against experimental benchmarks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Electronic contribution to the electrical conductivity, σML, versus the superconducting electron–phonon coupling constant λML for compounds in the Alexandria database, as predicted by machine-learning models. Blue points represent compounds with σ > 15 × 106 S/m, as identified by the fitted model.

FIG. 3. Electronic contribution to the electrical conductiv- ity, σBT, versus electron–phonon coupling λ for ∼12,800 com- pounds from the Alexandria Database and ∼1,200 additional ML-selected compounds, calculated using density functional theory with the PBEsol functional.
FIG. 4. Symbolic regression of the experimental conductivity as a function of the calculated variables σBT and λ.
